coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence mechanisms of red-emissive carbon dots derived from non-conjugated molecules
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Science China Press. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science China Press. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Science China Press. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science China Press.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xin
Ai, Lin
Yu, Jingkun
Waterhouse, Geoffrey I.N.
Sui, Laizhi
Ding, Jie
Zhang, Baowei
Yong, Xue
Lu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: Red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) have been widely studied because of their potential application in tissue imaging and optoelectronic devices. At present, most R-CDs are synthesized by using aromatic precursors, but the synthesis of R-CDs from non-aromatic precursors is challenging, and the emission mechanism remains unclear. Herein, different R-CDs were rationally synthesized using citric acid (CA), a prototype non-aromatic precursor, with the assistance of ammonia. Their structural evolution and optical mechanism were investigated. The addition of NH3·H2O played a key role in the synthesis of CA-based R-CDs, which shifted the emission wavelength of CA-based CDs from 423 to 667 nm. Mass spectrometry (MS) analysis indicated that the amino groups served as N dopants and promoted the formation of localized conjugated domains through an intermolecular amide ring, thereby inducing a significant emission redshift. The red-emissive mechanism of CDs was further confirmed by control experiments using other CA-like molecules (e.g., aconitic acid, tartaric acid, aspartic acid, malic acid, and maleic acid) as precursors. MS, nuclear magnetic resonance characterization, and computational modeling revealed that the main carbon chain length of CA-like precursors tailored the cyclization mode, leading to hexatomic, pentatomic, unstable three/four-membered ring systems or cyclization failure. Among these systems, the hexatomic ring led to the largest emission redshift (244 nm, known for CA-based CDs). This work determined the origin of red emission in CA-based CDs, which would guide research on the controlled synthesis of R-CDs from other non-aromatic precursors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence mechanism
Carbon dots
Citric acid
Aromatization
Red-emissive
Structural analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of other CA-like molecule-based CDs
Spectral analysis of the CA-B/RCDs growth mechanism of CA-RCDs
Universality of the large redshift of citrate-like CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (Color online) Schematic of the fabrication of CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs (a). CA-BCDs: TEM and HRTEM (b) and AFM (c). CA-RCDs: TEM and HRTEM (d) and AFM (e). CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs: XRD (f); FTIR spectra (g); XPS survey scans; and narrow scan C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s XPS spectra (h–k); 1H NMR (l); and mass spectra (m).

(Color online) Excitation-dependent spectra of CA-BCDs (a) and CA-RCDs (e); PL dynamics of CA-BCDs (b) and CA-RCDs (f); variable-temperature PL spectra of CA-BCDs (c, d) and CA-RCDs (g, h); 2D pseudocolor map of TA spectra expressed in ΔOD as functions of delay time and probe wavelength for CDs with a pump wavelength of 400 nm: CA-BCDs (i) and CA-RCDs (k); TA spectra of CA-BCDs (j) and CA-RCDs (l) at indicated delay time from 0.5 to 1000 ps.

(Color online) (a) Reaction routes from precursor molecules to a target molecule with m/z 508. (b) Second stage in the formation of CA-RCDs: dehydration aggregation. (c) Third stage in the formation of CA-RCDs: crosslinking carbonization. (d) DFT of the target molecular model and PL.
(Color online) TEM (a–e), AFM (f–j), 1H NMR (k), MS (l), and reaction scheme (m) of AC-RCDs, TA-RCDs, AA-RCDs, MI-RCDs, and ME-RCDs.

(Color online) (a–e) PL images of AC-BCDs, AC-RCDs, TA-BCDs, TA-RCDs, AA-BCDs, AA-RCDs MI-BCDs, MI-RCDs, ME-BCDs, and ME-RCDs. (f–j) DFT models for AC-RCDs, TA-RCDs, AA-RCDs, MI-RCDs, and ME-RCDs. (k–o) DFT emission of AC-RCDs, TA-RCDs, AA-RCDs, MI-RCDs, and ME-RCDs. (p) Schematic diagram of the formation of CDs through pyrolysis.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence mechanisms of red-emissive carbon dots derived from non-conjugated molecules

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Green Catalysis Center, and College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, The University of Auckland, Auckland 1142, New Zealand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, The University of Auckland, Auckland 1142, New Zealand

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Reaction Dynamics, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Sheffield, Sheffield S3 7HF, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Sheffield, Sheffield S3 7HF, UK

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) have been widely studied because of their potential application in tissue imaging and optoelectronic devices. At present, most R-CDs are synthesized by using aromatic precursors, but the synthesis of R-CDs from non-aromatic precursors is challenging, and the emission mechanism remains unclear. Herein, different R-CDs were rationally synthesized using citric acid (CA), a prototype non-aromatic precursor, with the assistance of ammonia. Their structural evolution and optical mechanism were investigated. The addition of NH3·H2O played a key role in the synthesis of CA-based R-CDs, which shifted the emission wavelength of CA-based CDs from 423 to 667 nm. Mass spectrometry (MS) analysis indicated that the amino groups served as N dopants and promoted the formation of localized conjugated domains through an intermolecular amide ring, thereby inducing a significant emission redshift. The red-emissive mechanism of CDs was further confirmed by control experiments using other CA-like molecules (e.g., aconitic acid, tartaric acid, aspartic acid, malic acid, and maleic acid) as precursors. MS, nuclear magnetic resonance characterization, and computational modeling revealed that the main carbon chain length of CA-like precursors tailored the cyclization mode, leading to hexatomic, pentatomic, unstable three/four-membered ring systems or cyclization failure. Among these systems, the hexatomic ring led to the largest emission redshift (244 nm, known for CA-based CDs). This work determined the origin of red emission in CA-based CDs, which would guide research on the controlled synthesis of R-CDs from other non-aromatic precursors.

ce:section.ce:para: Red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) show great potential in a wide range of applications [1–8], particularly in biomedical fields, because of their good biocompatibility, deep-tissue penetration ability, and weak autofluorescence [9–11]. R-CDs usually contain a large conjugated carbon core; thus, aromatic molecules are commonly used as precursors during their synthesis [12]. For example, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) is a commonly used precursor of R-CD synthesis [13–15]. In recent years, the synthesis and emission mechanism of OPD-based R-CDs have been systematically investigated [16–18]. By contrast, the polymerization process for generating R-CDs from non-aromatic precursors is more complex, and the structure of the obtained R-CDs is not well-defined [19,20]. Accordingly, few systematic studies have explored the evolution of R-CDs from non-aromatic precursors [21–24].

Citric acid (CA) is a potential non-aromatic precursor [25–30]. Given its abundant functional groups (–CO, –(CO)-OH) and reactive sites, CA can easily form fluorescent carbon nanostructures through crosslinking and carbonization [16,31]. In constructing CA-based R-CDs, the addition of amine derivatives is necessary, such as urea [32–35], thiourea [36], ethylenediamine [37], acrylamide [38], polyethyleneimine [39], and 1,4,5,8-tetraminoanthraquinone [40], all of which contain active amino groups. Three strategies could be used to generate the fluorescence of CA-based R-CDs [41]. The first strategy is the construction of large sp 2 domains. CA and urea generate R-CDs through solvothermal reaction in a polar aprotic solvent of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) [35]. Second, Li et al. [7] utilized molecules or polymers with a sulfoxide/carbonyl group (i.e., containing SO and CO groups) to modify the surface of CDs. Electron-deficient SO and CO groups can bind to the outer layers of CDs and induce surface oxidation, leading to the bending of the outer layers of CDs and generating near-infrared (NIR) emission. Lastly, heteroatom doping can vary the electronic structure of carbon core and surface states of CDs, thereby controlling the optical properties. Jiang et al. [42] synthesized N,F-co-doped CDs by solvothermal reaction of CA, urea, and ammonium fluoride in DMF. The obtained N,F-co-doped CDs had a NIR emission band centered at 777 nm upon excitation at ∼700 nm. However, the photoluminescence (PL) mechanism in CA-based R-CDs remains unclear because of the polydispersity of products and the difficulty in determining their chemical structures. The lack of knowledge on the structure–function relationship of CA-based R-CDs hinders the design of advanced R-CDs from non-aromatic molecules.

In this work, six non-aromatic precursors (CA and five CA derivatives with different chain lengths) were used to produce R-CDs in the addition of ammonia. The chain length of the CA-based precursor played an important role in the structural and optical properties of R-CDs. Mass spectrometry (MS), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) characterization, and computational modeling revealed that the chain length of the CA-based precursor determined the type of cyclic structure formed in the presence of amine groups during R-CDs evolution. Such cyclic structure played a key role in the redshift emission of CDs. In particular, the heterocyclic six-membered ring system resulted in the maximum redshift. This work provides valuable insights into the structural evolution of R-CDs from non-aromatic precursors.
Aconitic acid (AC), CA, tartaric acid (TA), aspartic acid (AA), malic acid (MI), maleic acid (ME), ammonia water (NH3·H2O), DMF, and all chemical reagents were used without further purification.

0.96 g of CA was dissolved in 10 mL of DMF and then transferred to a 25 mL liner. 1.5 mL of H2O was added dropwise into the liner and then sonicated for 30 min. The liner was placed in an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, a dialysis bag (500 Da) was used for 8 h to remove DMF, unreacted precursors, and other small intermediate molecules to obtain high-purity CDs. The synthesis of CA-RCDs was similar to the abovementioned protocols except for replacing H2O with NH3·H2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of other CA-like molecule-based CDs
ce:section.ce:para: AC, TA, AA, MI, and ME were used as carbon sources. Carbon sources were dissolved in 10 mL of DMF with a concentration of 0.005 mmol L−1. These solutions were transferred to five liners and sonicated for 30 min following the dropwise addition of 1.5 mL of H2O. The mixtures were then placed in an autoclave, numbered as 1–5, and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, a dialysis bag (500 Da) was used for 8 h to remove DMF, unreacted precursor molecules, and other small intermediate molecules to obtain five different BCDs (AC-BCDs, TA-BCDs, AA-BCDs, MI-BCDs, and ME-BCDs). If 1.5 mL of H2O was replaced by 1.5 mL of NH3·H2O based on the abovementioned protocols, then five different RCDs (AC-RCDs, TA-RCDs, AA-RCDs, MI-RCDs, and ME-RCDs) with redshifted emission would be obtained.

Blue-emissive CDs (CA-BCDs) were synthesized using CA as carbon source and DMF as solvent. Red-emissive CDs (CA-RCDs) were obtained when NH3·H2O was added as nitrogen source (Fig. 1 a). The structures of CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs were investigated. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis showed that CDs had a uniform size and good dispersibility. The average particle size of CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs was 3.34 and 3.26 nm, respectively [43]. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) revealed that both CDs had a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1b and d), corresponding to (100) facets of graphitic carbon. Subsequently, atomic force microscopy (AFM) showed that CDs had a height of 3–5 nm (Fig. 1c and e), which was consistent with the HRTEM result. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns for both samples showed a broad peak at 25.6° (Fig. 1f), which is a typical feature for CDs. Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), 1H NMR, and MS analyses were performed to probe compositional differences between CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs. FTIR provided information about functional groups on the surface of CDs (Fig. 1g) [44]. CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs showed similar absorption peaks at 3425, 1620, and 1403 cm−1, corresponding to –OH/–NH, CO, and CN group vibrations, respectively. However, CA-RCDs showed additional FTIR peaks at 1706 and 1097 cm−1, corresponding to –CO-NH– and C-NH groups, respectively. The exact elemental composition of CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs was analyzed by XPS (Fig. 1h) [45]. CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs samples showed specific signals of three elements, including C, N, and O (note: H is undetectable by XPS). Both samples contained N because of the involvement of DMF solvent in their synthesis, whereas the N content of CA-RCDs (2.85%) was more than that of CA-BCDs (1.35%). The increase of N content for CA-RCDs indicated an enhanced conjugation, possibly because of the addition of ammonia in the synthesis process. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra of samples were deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 1i), corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, and CO. The percentage of C–C/CC:C–O/C–N:CO content for CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs was 65.7:14.1:20.1 and 73.7:15.5:10.8, respectively, indicating a higher graphitization degree of CA-RCDs than CA-BCDs (Table S1 online). In addition, the high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra of CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs showed four peaks corresponding to pyridinic N (389.9 eV), amino N (399.8 eV), pyrrolic N (400.6 eV), and graphitic N (401.5 eV; Fig. 1j). CA-BCDs were rich in pyridinic N, whereas CA-RCDs contained more amino N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N species. The high-resolution O 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 1k) showed oxygen in the sample in two environments, CO (531.5 eV) and C–O (533.1 eV).

The 1H NMR spectra of CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs are shown in Fig. 1k. The signals at 1.0 and 1.8 ppm were assigned to alkane CH, and the triplet peak at 2.6–2.9 ppm corresponded to benzylic α-H. In addition, the signal at 3.5 ppm corresponded to α-H (amino), and the signal at 5.38 ppm corresponded to amide H. These non-aromatic hydrogens should be located on the surface of CDs [1]. Peaks at 6.51 and 7.82 ppm correspond to aromatic H. In particular, the peak at 8.34 ppm only appeared in the CA-RCDs sample, which corresponded to aromatic rings with an armchair structure (Fig. 1l). The XPS and NMR results indicated that more polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon structures could be found in CA-RCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral analysis of the CA-B/RCDs growth mechanism of CA-RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Different speciation of N in CDs could induce different fluorescence properties [46,47]. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the maximum emission peak for CA-BCDs was at 423 nm with a PLQY of 3.68%. For CA-RCDs, the maximum emission peak was at 667 nm with a PLQY of 60.73%, which was the largest redshift and the highest PLQY value (Table S2 online). Nitrogen atoms strongly affected the optical properties of CDs. Graphitized N existed in the sp 2 domain of the carbon core, and its two non-pair electrons could increase the electron density of the entire π-conjugated domain, thereby reducing the non-radiative recombination center of CDs. XPS showed that CA-RCDs contained more graphitic N atoms than CA-BCDs, with the former showing greatly enhanced optical emission properties (which is consistent with theoretical calculations). Then, we studied the steady-state PL lifetime of CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs. The fitting results showed that the PL decay curves of CDs followed a dual­exponential formula, involving a short­lived component τ 1 and a long­lived component τ 2. These two components could be assigned to the recombination of the carbon core and surface state, respectively. The average PL lifetime of CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs was 11.445 and 5.1164 ns, respectively (Fig. 2c and d), and τ 1 of CA-RCDs was longer than that of CA-BCDs. This result indicated that the core state played a key role in the PL redshift of CA-RCDs.

Subsequently, temperature (200–290 K) dependent fluorescence spectra of CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs were collected by variable-temperature PL spectroscopy (Fig. 2c and g). For both samples, the PL intensity gradually decreased with the increase in temperature because of the increased probability of non-radiative recombination. Each emission peak decayed at a specific rate, indicating the presence of two different PL centers (Fig. 2d and h). We performed femtosecond transient absorption with 400 nm laser pulse (Fig. 2i and k) and scan delay times ranging from −1 to 1000 ps (Fig. 2j and l) to investigate the carrier relaxation dynamic and luminescence mechanism of the two types of CDs. The positive absorption peaks of CA-BCDs at 440–610 nm and 710–800 nm corresponded to the excited state absorption (ESA) of charge-separated states after photoexcitation. The intensity of the positive absorption peaks at 471 and 753 nm gradually decreased with delay time, which could be attributed to the weakening of the ground-state bleaching properties associated with transitions between the valence band and electron-poor trap states. The positive absorption of CA-RCDs at 671–800 nm corresponded to the ESA of the charge-separated state, which was formed from the excited state after photoexcitation. The negative signal at 440–671 nm corresponded to the stimulated emission (SE). The SE decay time of CA-RCDs CA-BCDs was different, indicating their different fluorescence origins.

MS was used to analyze the fragments in the CDs structure and reveal the growth and fluorescence mechanism (Fig. 1 m). In the spectrum of CA-RCDs, m/z 130 was the ring formed by the amidation reaction between CA and NH3·H2O; m/z 171 was the citrate derived from the reaction of CA and NH3·H2O; m/z 207 belonged to the two-membered ring molecule derived from the further reaction of m/z 130 with unreacted CA and NH3·H2O. Mass fragments from 272, 286, 300, 383, 397, 411, 494 to 508 corresponded to the further polymerized structure (Fig. S1 online). Based on MS analysis, the structural models for CA-BCDs and CA-RCDs were constructed with molecular weights of 193 and 508, respectively. The value of m/z 508 was established from MS of CA-RCDs (Supplementary materials) and matched well with the reaction scheme of CA-based amidation and condensation reactions (Fig. 3 a). Then, the rationality of the models was explored using density functional theory (DFT) calculation [48]. The computed emission peak of the CA-BCD model at 428 nm (Fig. S3 online) was similar to the experimental results (423 nm). Similarly, the calculated fluorescence emission peak of the CA-RCDs model was at 660 nm, which was consistent with the experimental results (667 nm, Fig. 3d). Studying the reaction progress, we hypothesized that the formation of CA-RCDs occurred in three stages: first, CA and NH3·H2O reacted to form a six-membered ring molecule through an amidation reaction, corresponding to the m/z 130 fragment. The conjugated domain of the molecular structure further expanded through intermolecular amination, deacidification, and intermolecular dehydration, creating a molecule with m/z 508 (RM). In the second stage, the m/z 508 molecule and intermediate products of the reaction were further dehydrated and polymerized to form a polymer (Fig. 3b). In the third stage, the polymer and molecules in the system were further dehydrated and carbonized into CDs. Based on the high-resolution mass spectrum, a series of ion fragments was found in the range of m/z 600–1000 (at m/z 615, 689, 763, 837, 911, 985) separated by 74 mass units (Fig. S2 online). Mass differences corresponded to –CH2COOCH3. We hypothesized that these groups existed on the surface of CDs formed by the carbonization of aggregates, which determined the fluorescence properties of CDs (Fig. 3c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Universality of the large redshift of citrate-like CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we extended the synthesis method to other non-aromatic and organic small-molecule precursors of R-CDs. We found that aromatic molecules or organic molecules with carbon backbones less than four Cs could not produce CDs with a redshift even in the presence of NH3·H2O. However, organic molecules with the main carbon chain greater than (or equal to) four Cs and more than three reaction sites on the carbon chain yielded CDs with a large redshift (when NH3·H2O was also used as a reagent). We hypothesized that aromatic rings could be buckled sufficiently when reacted with ammonia and could induce a redshift, whereas organic molecules with less than four Cs in their carbon backbones could not form such stable ring structures.

We performed structural analyses on CDs prepared from AC, TA, AA, MI, and ME to understand the origin of this phenomenon. TEM images showed that all ten samples, which were prepared with and without NH3·H2O, had a quasi-spherical shape and an average size of 2.75 nm (Fig. 4 a–e and Fig. S4 online). HRTEM revealed they all samples had a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon. Subsequently, AFM images of the samples revealed that the particle size ranged from 3 to 5 nm, which was in good agreement with the TEM results (Fig. 4f–j and Fig. S5 online). Among the five precursors, CDs were primarily in the form of five-membered and six-membered rings because of different carbon chain lengths under the introduction of NH3·H2O. 1H NMR proton shifts for five-membered rings in CDs occurred in the range of 6.235–6.737 ppm, whereas the proton shifts on the six-membered ring occurred in the range of 7.38–7.75 and 8.59 ppm (Fig. 4k). FTIR and XPS analyses were used to compare the composition and structure of AC-RCDs, TA-RCDs, AA-RCDs, MI-RCDs, and ME-RCDs. FTIR and XPS data indicated that the addition of NH3·H2O remarkably changed N-containing functional groups, with the appearance of –CO-NH– and –NH2 groups (Fig. S6 online). The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra (Figs. S7 and S8) showed that BCDs were rich in pyridine nitrogen, whereas RCDs contained more amino nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen, and graphitic nitrogen. Furthermore, by analyzing the MS results (Fig. 4m and l), the reaction routes of five CA analogs were deduced, and the corresponding structural models for each type of CD were established.

Then, we explored the optical properties of CDs. The CDs prepared from five precursor molecules in the presence of NH3·H2O showed more than 100 nm emission redshift compared with the corresponding CDs prepared without NH3·H2O (Fig. 5 a–e). We performed DFT calculations using the molecular models to verify the rationality of such models created for each type of CD (Fig. 5b–j). The theoretically calculated PL emission maxima for AC-RCDs, TA-RCDs, AA-RCDs, MI-RCDs, and ME-RCDs models were 646, 606, 604, 599, and 598 nm, respectively (Fig. 5k–o). These theoretically calculated values were in good agreement with experimental data, which supported the universality of the reaction route.

R-CDs with recorded PLQY (60.73%) were prepared by the hydrothermal reaction of CA with the addition of NH3·H2O. Analysis revealed that the addition of NH3·H2O could enhance aromatization, leading to the formation of large, conjugated domains through intermolecular cyclization between CA and NH3·H2O. Cyclization was formed in the carbon core, and the degree of buckling of conjugated rings resulted in the large emission redshift and suppressed non-radiative recombination. Five CA analogs (AC, TA, AA, MI, and ME) with different carbon chain lengths were also used to synthesize CDs, with six-membered heterocyclic rings inducing the maximum redshift (244 nm), followed by five-membered rings (182 nm). The structural model of each CD system was theoretically proposed, whose computed PL emission maxima matched well with experimental data. This work provided new insights into the fluorescence mechanism of CA-based CDs, guiding the development of novel high-performance R-CDs from non-aromatic precursors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52122308, 21905253, 51973200, and 52103239), Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (202300410372), and Henan Postdoctoral Foundation.

Xin Yang contributed to the synthesis and characterization. Xin Yang, Lin Ai, Laizhi Sui, Geoffrey I. N. Waterhouse, Jie Ding, and Baowei Zhang wrote the manuscript. Jingkun Yu and Xue Yong performed the computational calculation. Siyu Lu conceived the idea and organized the manuscript. All the authors contributed to the discussion.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary materials to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scib.2022.06.013.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly luminescent nitrogen-doped nanographene as an acid- and metal-sensitive fluorophore for optical imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jin E, Yang Q, Ju C, et al. A highly luminescent nitrogen-doped nanographene as an acid- and metal-sensitive fluorophore for optical imaging. J Am Chem Soc 2021;143:10403-10412.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu S, Meng Q, Wang L, et al. Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging. Angew Chem Int Ed 2013;52:3953-3957.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ photoconversion of multicolor luminescence and pure white light emission based on carbon dot-supported supramolecular assembly

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wu H, Chen Y, Dai X, et al. In situ photoconversion of multicolor luminescence and pure white light emission based on carbon dot-supported supramolecular assembly. J Am Chem Soc 2019;141:6583-6591.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots in zeolites: a new class of thermally activated delayed fluorescence materials with ultralong lifetimes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu J, Wang N, Yu Y, et al. Carbon dots in zeolites: a new class of thermally activated delayed fluorescence materials with ultralong lifetimes. Sci Adv 2017;3:e1603171

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Deep red emissive carbonized polymer dots with unprecedented narrow full width at half maximum

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu J, Geng Y, Li D, et al. Deep red emissive carbonized polymer dots with unprecedented narrow full width at half maximum. Adv Mater 2020;32:1906641.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toward efficient orange emissive carbon nanodots through conjugated sp2 -domain controlling and surface charges engineering

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Qu S, Zhou D, Li D, et al. Toward efficient orange emissive carbon nanodots through conjugated sp2 -domain controlling and surface charges engineering. Adv Mater 2016;28:3516-3521.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared excitation/emission and multiphoton-induced fluorescence of carbon dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphitic nitrogen triggers red fluorescence in carbon dots

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xin Yang is a postgraduate student at the College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. Her research focuses on the mechanism and application of red-emissive carbon dot fluorescence.

Lin Ai is a post-doctor at the College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. She received her Ph.D. degree in Polymer Physics and Chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Hao Zhang at Jilin University. Her research interest is related to the novel fluorescent nanomaterials, focusing on polymer carbon dots and metal nanoclusters.

Siyu Lu is currently a professor and top young talent at the College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. He received his Ph.D. degree in Polymer Physics and Chemistry in 2016 at Jilin University. His research interest is related to carbon-dot-based functional nanomaterials and the controllable preparation of carbon dots.
